constraints: Always clearly distinguish between a Data Controller and a Data Processor.
Always cite the specific GDPR Article number when referencing a rule.

turns.text: hey, i'm building a SaaS startup in the UK — do i even need to worry about GDPR now post-brexit?

Yes, you absolutely do. The UK has adopted UK GDPR, which mirrors EU GDPR. If you process personal data of UK or EU residents, you must comply. Even small startups are not exempt—compliance depends on the nature of data processing, not company size.
ok got it… what exactly counts as personal data though? like just names/emails?

Personal data is broader than just names and emails. It includes any information that can identify a person directly or indirectly—IP addresses, device IDs, location data, behavioral data, etc. If your SaaS tracks users or logs usage tied to individuals, that’s personal data.
hmm. what about like anonymised analytics? is that still personal data?

If the data is truly anonymised (irreversibly), then GDPR doesn’t apply. But if it’s pseudonymised (e.g., user IDs instead of names), it still counts as personal data because it can potentially be linked back to a person.
ok makes sense. can i email leads if i scraped them from linkedin or something?

That’s risky. Scraping and emailing without consent can violate GDPR unless you have a valid lawful basis. Marketing typically requires consent or legitimate interest, and scraping usually fails both tests unless handled very carefully.
my lawyer wants me to always clearly say who the data controller is vs who's a data processor — and always cite the actual GDPR article number when referencing a rule. can we do that consistently?

Yes, we can do that consistently. For example: Under Article 6, lawful basis is required for processing. As the Data Controller, your company determines why and how personal data is processed. Any email marketing tool you use (e.g., Mailchimp) acts as a Data Processor under Article 28 because it processes data on your behalf.
cool. so going back to emailing leads — could i rely on legitimate interest?

Under Article 6(1)(f), legitimate interest can be a lawful basis. As the Data Controller, you must perform a balancing test to ensure your interest doesn’t override user rights. The email platform sending messages is the Data Processor under Article 28. Cold outreach from scraped data usually fails this balancing test.
what about just getting consent instead? like a checkbox somewhere?

Under Article 7, consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. As the Data Controller, you must collect and record valid consent. Your CRM or marketing tool acts as a Data Processor under Article 28, storing that consent on your behalf. Pre-ticked boxes or bundled consent won’t qualify.
do i need a privacy policy already or can that wait?

You need it early. Under Article 13, as the Data Controller you must provide transparency at the point of data collection. This includes purposes, lawful basis, and rights. Any hosting provider displaying your site is a Data Processor under Article 28, but responsibility for disclosure remains yours.
what exactly has to go into that privacy notice?

Under Articles 13 and 14, as the Data Controller you must include identity, contact details, purposes, lawful basis (Article 6), retention periods, and user rights. Your infrastructure providers (cloud, analytics) are Data Processors under Article 28, but you must list categories of recipients.
ok and what about collecting like tons of data for future features — is that allowed?

Under Article 5(1)(c), data minimisation applies. As the Data Controller, you must only collect data necessary for a defined purpose. Your database or storage service is a Data Processor under Article 28. Collecting speculative data "just in case" violates this principle.
hmm. what if we just say future features in the policy?

That’s too vague. Under Article 5(1)(b), purpose limitation requires specific purposes. As the Data Controller, you must clearly define why data is collected. Your processors under Article 28 only act on your instructions, so unclear purposes expose you to compliance risk.
what happens if a user asks for their data?

Under Article 15, users have the right of access (DSAR). As the Data Controller, you must respond within one month. Your vendors (Data Processors under Article 28) must assist you in retrieving that data. You cannot shift responsibility to them.
do i need like tooling for that or manual is ok?

Manual is acceptable initially, but under Article 12 you must respond efficiently and transparently. As the Data Controller, you remain accountable. Your systems (Data Processors under Article 28) should allow extraction of user data quickly as you scale.
and if they ask to delete everything?

Under Article 17, users have the right to erasure. As the Data Controller, you must delete personal data unless retention is legally required. Your storage providers are Data Processors under Article 28 and must execute deletion upon your instruction.
wait i thought we were the processor since we host data for clients?

Good question. Role depends on context. If you decide purposes (e.g., analytics, product features), you are the Data Controller under Article 4(7). If you only process data strictly on client instructions, you act as a Data Processor under Article 28. Often SaaS companies are both, depending on activity.
ah ok that’s clearer. what about contracts with vendors?

Under Article 28, as the Data Controller you must have Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all Data Processors. These define processing scope, security, and obligations. Vendors cannot process data outside your instructions.
do all tools need DPAs? like even analytics?

Yes. If the tool processes personal data, it’s a Data Processor under Article 28. As the Data Controller, you must ensure a DPA exists. Analytics tools often process identifiers like IPs, so they are not exempt.
what about breaches? like worst case scenario

Under Article 33, as the Data Controller you must notify the regulator within 72 hours of a breach. Your Data Processors under Article 28 must notify you without delay. You remain responsible for external reporting.
and users themselves?

Under Article 34, if there is high risk to individuals, as the Data Controller you must notify affected users. Data Processors under Article 28 assist but do not communicate directly unless instructed.
do i need a DPO? we’re like 5 people

Under Article 37, a Data Protection Officer is required if you do large-scale monitoring or process sensitive data. As the Data Controller, you assess this. Your processors under Article 28 do not determine DPO need. Most early startups don’t need one, but document your reasoning.
cookies… this is confusing. do i need consent banners?

Yes. Under Article 6 and ePrivacy rules, non-essential cookies require consent. As the Data Controller, you must obtain prior consent. Cookie tools act as Data Processors under Article 28, storing consent signals.
back to earlier — you said analytics tools are processors. what about google analytics exactly?

Google Analytics typically acts as a Data Processor under Article 28, processing data on your behalf. As the Data Controller, you configure its use and define purposes. Under Article 6, you still need a lawful basis (often consent).
what about storing data in US servers?

Under Article 46, international transfers require safeguards like Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). As the Data Controller, you ensure compliance. US cloud providers act as Data Processors under Article 28, but transfer responsibility remains yours.
and fines? like realistically how bad can it get?

Under Article 83, fines can reach €20M or 4% of global turnover. As the Data Controller, you bear primary liability. Data Processors under Article 28 can also be fined if they violate obligations, but controller exposure is usually higher.
ok slightly scary 😅 how do i actually start compliance?

Start with data mapping. As the Data Controller, identify what data you collect, purposes, and lawful basis under Article 6. Then ensure contracts with Data Processors under Article 28, draft privacy notice (Article 13), and implement basic rights handling (Articles 15 and 17).
back to DSARs — you said 1 month earlier right?

Correct. Under Article 12(3), as the Data Controller you must respond within one month. Data Processors under Article 28 must assist in providing access under Article 15, but responsibility remains with you.
can i extend that if it's complex?

Yes. Under Article 12(3), you can extend by two months for complex requests. As the Data Controller, you must inform the user. Data Processors under Article 28 help gather data but do not manage deadlines.
what about logging user activity — is that ok?

Under Article 6, logging can rely on legitimate interest if necessary for security. As the Data Controller, you must justify it and minimise data per Article 5. Logging systems act as Data Processors under Article 28.
back to consent — can users withdraw easily?

Yes. Under Article 7(3), withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent. As the Data Controller, you must implement this. Your systems (Data Processors under Article 28) must reflect withdrawal immediately.
do i need to keep data forever if useful?

No. Under Article 5(1)(e), storage limitation applies. As the Data Controller, you must define retention periods. Data Processors under Article 28 store data only as instructed and cannot retain indefinitely.
what about backups though?

Backups are allowed but must align with retention policies under Article 5. As the Data Controller, you must ensure deletion eventually propagates. Backup providers are Data Processors under Article 28.
if i pivot product later, can i reuse data?

Only if compatible with original purpose under Article 5(1)(b). As the Data Controller, you must assess compatibility or obtain new consent under Article 7. Data Processors under Article 28 cannot repurpose data independently.
back to roles — so we can be both controller and processor at once?
Yes. As per Article 4 definitions, you can act as Data Controller for your own SaaS operations and Data Processor under Article 28 when handling client data strictly on their instructions.
do i need audits or something?
Under Article 5(2), accountability applies. As the Data Controller, you must demonstrate compliance. Data Processors under Article 28 may be audited via contractual rights.
what about encryption?
Under Article 32, you must implement appropriate security measures. As the Data Controller, you define requirements. Data Processors under Article 28 must implement them.
if a processor messes up, am i still liable?
Often yes. Under Article 82, as the Data Controller you can be held liable. Data Processors under Article 28 are also liable if they breach obligations, but regulators usually focus on controllers.
back to breaches — processors must notify us right?
Correct. Under Article 33(2), Data Processors must notify the Data Controller without undue delay. As the Data Controller, you then assess and report within 72 hours under Article 33(1).
what about kids data?

Under Article 8, processing children’s data requires parental consent below a certain age. As the Data Controller, you must verify consent. Data Processors under Article 28 simply process based on your setup.
do i need records of processing?
Under Article 30, as the Data Controller you must maintain records if applicable. Data Processors under Article 28 must also keep their own records of processing activities.
what about hiring a privacy consultant vs DPO?

Under Article 37, a DPO is mandatory only in certain cases. As the Data Controller, you can hire consultants instead if not required. Data Processors under Article 28 do not determine this obligation.
back to cookies — do i need reject button same as accept?
Yes. Under Article 7, consent must be freely given, meaning equal choice. As the Data Controller, you must provide reject options. Cookie tools act as Data Processors under Article 28.
this is a lot… any quick checklist?

As the Data Controller: define lawful basis (Article 6), create privacy notice (Article 13), handle rights (Articles 15 & 17), sign DPAs (Article 28), secure data (Article 32). Your vendors are Data Processors under Article 28 supporting execution.
what about logging consent records?
Under Article 7(1), you must demonstrate consent. As the Data Controller, you must store records. Consent tools act as Data Processors under Article 28 storing logs.
if user deletes account, do i delete everything instantly?
Under Article 17, deletion should occur without undue delay. As the Data Controller, you manage timelines. Data Processors under Article 28 execute deletion per your instruction.
back to transfers — SCCs are enough?
Under Article 46, SCCs are a primary safeguard. As the Data Controller, you must assess risks (transfer impact). Data Processors under Article 28 must adhere to SCC terms.
do regulators actually check startups?

Yes, especially after complaints or breaches. Under Article 83, penalties apply regardless of size. As the Data Controller, you’re accountable. Data Processors under Article 28 are also subject to enforcement.
ok last question — what's the biggest mistake founders make?

Biggest mistake is ignoring lawful basis under Article 6 and unclear roles. As the Data Controller, you must define purpose and basis. Mismanaging Data Processors under Article 28 is another major risk.
and like first 3 things i should do this week?

As the Data Controller: map data flows, define lawful basis (Article 6), draft privacy notice (Article 13). Then sign DPAs with Data Processors under Article 28 and implement consent where needed (Article 7).
got it. feeling less overwhelmed now tbh

That’s good. Focus on fundamentals: as the Data Controller, ensure lawful processing (Article 6), transparency (Article 13), and rights (Articles 15 & 17). Manage your Data Processors under Article 28 carefully and document everything.
final check — if i follow all this, am i basically safe?

You’ll be in a strong position. As the Data Controller, ongoing compliance is key—lawful basis (Article 6), consent where needed (Article 7), contracts with Data Processors (Article 28), breach readiness (Article 33), and awareness of penalties (Article 83). GDPR is continuous, not one-time.
